one_line_takeaway: Cross-validated/averaged logistic-regression models like the publicly deployed LORIS clinical tool leak training-cohort membership almost perfectly under white-box access (and are attackable directly via its live web interface), and tensorizing models -- including discretizing outputs before tensorization -- reduces this leakage to near-random while costing far less accuracy than Differential Privacy and simultaneously improving model interpretability.
key_finding: Table 1 (Hamming/multi-label attack accuracy, mean+-std over repeated CV): unprotected vanilla LR reaches 0.8178 (2-WBB), 0.9129 (SBB), 0.9330 (WB); the cross-validation-averaged LR (LORIS's actual deployment practice) is markedly more vulnerable, reaching 0.9149/0.9910/0.9999 -- essentially perfect white-box identification. Unprotected NN reaches 0.7375/0.8608/0.6336 (WB attacks on NNs underperform BB attacks, unlike LR, illustrating NNs are harder to attack via parameters). TT-LR at b=2 drops attack scores to 0.6666 (2-WBB) / 0.8231 (SBB) / 0.7461 (WB, via reconstructed LR coefficients), comparable to LR-DP at epsilon=10 (0.7163/0.7840/0.6403) but with much better retained accuracy than DP. TT-NN at b=2 achieves 0.5759/0.6184/0.5061 -- white-box attacks on TT-NN are reduced to ~50% (random guessing) because NN parameters cannot be reconstructed from TT outputs, unlike LR. In the targeted Cho1-vs-Cho1+Kato case study (Table 2), the averaged LR achieves near-perfect Kato-cohort identification under white-box access (1.0000) and ~92% under SBB, while vanilla LR under WB still reaches ~71%; TT-LR (b=2) and TT-NN remain near-random (0.55 and 0.52 WB respectively) across all access levels. Direct attack on the real, publicly deployed LORIS web interface (Table 3): using coefficients reconstructed purely from the online interface's rounded probability outputs, the white-box attack correctly identifies Cho1 as the training set with 0.9944 probability (vs. 1.0000 using the officially released coefficients), demonstrating the attack works against the actual live clinical tool, not just a lab replica. Feature-sensitivity scores computed via TT marginalization align almost perfectly with LR coefficients after normalization (Fig. 1), and cancer-type-conditioned TT-LR models reveal subgroup-specific effects invisible to the (per-feature-constant) LR model, e.g. all pancreatic-cancer patients in Cho1 are non-responders so the conditioned model finds no feature informative for that subgroup despite 100% accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is (1) an empirical membership-inference-style attack that determines which public patient cohort datasets were used to train a clinical prediction model, evaluated under three access levels (b-bin discretized black-box, strong/continuous black-box, and white-box parameter access), applied to LORIS (a real, publicly deployed logistic-regression model for immunotherapy response prediction) and to comparable shallow neural networks; and (2) a novel defense that tensorizes trained models (LR and NN) into quantum-inspired tensor train (TT) representations using the TT-RSS distillation procedure, additionally tensorizing *discretized* output scores (rather than raw scores) to gain black-box protection, combined with gauge randomization of TT cores to obfuscate white-box parameters. The authors show this reduces white-box attacks to near-random guessing while degrading black-box attacks to a level comparable to Differential Privacy, with substantially less accuracy loss than DP-SGD/DP-LR. They also demonstrate that TT models retain and extend LR interpretability (closed-form marginal/conditional distributions enabling feature-sensitivity analysis and cancer-type-specific conditioning) and that this interpretability benefit extends to tensorized NNs, which are otherwise black-box.
threat_model: Adversary knows the model architecture, the finite universe of candidate public training datasets, and the finite hyperparameter search space used by the defender; the adversary can train unlimited shadow models under the same training procedure. Attacker access is categorized into three independent tiers: (1) b-Weak Black-Box (b-WBB) -- only outputs discretized into b bins (b=2 is a binary output); (2) Strong Black-Box (SBB) -- raw continuous prediction scores on chosen samples; (3) White-Box (WB) -- full model parameters. The attack is a multi-label membership-style classifier (an MLP meta-classifier) trained on shadow-model outputs/parameters to predict, per candidate dataset, whether it was included in the target's training set. The paper explicitly notes this is coarser than per-record membership inference but argues that in medical settings a 'public dataset' may correspond to a very small, identifiable patient cohort (e.g., the 35-patient Kato cohort), so dataset-level leakage can still be clinically/legally sensitive.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (i) the b-WBB/SBB/WB access categories are treated as independent for controlled analysis, but a real adversary combining both BB and WB access could mount stronger attacks than reported; (ii) for LR, WB parameters can theoretically be recovered exactly from SBB scores via the model's monotonicity, but in practice this requires evaluations at specific/additional samples, so the reported SBB attacks sometimes underperform WB attacks despite theoretical equivalence -- an acknowledged gap between theory and the empirical attack's actual data efficiency; (iii) larger b (finer output discretization) improves TT predictive accuracy only modestly but sharply increases attack success, so the b=2 vs. larger-b tradeoff is somewhat brittle and requires empirical tuning; (iv) a rigorous DP-style epsilon guarantee for the discretization-based BB defense is not established -- the authors state 'a direct one-to-one comparison [with DP] is not possible without DP-style output perturbation' and describe discretization as only 'analogous' to noise injection; (v) TT-LR white-box attacks still work somewhat better than random guessing (0.75-0.81) because LR coefficients can be partially reconstructed from TT evaluations, whereas TT-NN achieves near-perfect WB obfuscation only because NN parameters are not recoverable this way -- the defense's WB strength is architecture-dependent; (vi) evaluated only on one clinical prediction task (immunotherapy response, LORIS) and shallow model classes (LR, 2-layer MLP), so generalization to other clinical tasks or deeper architectures is not empirically demonstrated within the paper.

datasets_used: Cho1 (n=964, Chowell et al. 2022 train partition)
Cho2 (n=515, Chowell et al. 2022 test partition)
quantitative_results: Unprotected averaged (CV-deployed) LR: WB attack Hamming score 0.9999 +/- 0.0000 (near-perfect training-set identification)
LR-DP epsilon=10: WB 0.6403 +/- 0.0149, SBB 0.7840 +/- 0.0140, 2-WBB 0.7163 +/- 0.0087 (roughly comparable to TT-LR b=2 but with worse utility per paper's claim)
Cho1-vs-Cho1+Kato case study: averaged LR WB attack accuracy 1.0000 +/- 0.0000 identifying the 35-patient Kato cohort; SBB 0.9182 +/- 0.1490; vanilla LR WB ~0.7141 +/- 0.0245
Cho1-vs-Cho1+Kato: TT-LR (b=2) WB 0.5521 +/- 0.0208, TT-NN (b=2) WB 0.5152 +/- 0.0765 (near random for both)

Real LORIS web-interface attack: WB attack assigns Cho1 probability 0.9944 using coefficients reconstructed purely from the public online interface (vs. 1.0000 using officially released coefficients), correctly identifying the true training dataset
baselines_compared: Differential Privacy for LR (DP-LR via Diffprivlib, epsilon in {0.1,1,10,100})
DP-SGD for NN (via Opacus, sigma in {20,5,1,0}, approx epsilon in {0.2,1,10,infinity})
Unprotected cross-validation-averaged LR (the actual LORIS deployment procedure)
